elements.sent-2411-001: We study the adjoint cohomology of perfect Lie algebras over the complex numbers.
elements.sent-2411-002: For the family of perfect Lie algebras $\Lg=\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)\ltimes V_m$ we obtain some explicit results for $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ with $k\ge 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Here $V_m$ is the irreducible representation of $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)$ of dimension $m+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-004: For the computation of the cohomology we use the Hochschild-Serre formula, a long exact sequence in the cohomology and explicit formulas for the multiplicities of $V_k$ in the exterior product $\Lambda^j(V_m)$ for $j\le 4$.
elements.sent-2411-005: In general we cannot determine the total adjoint cohomology for $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)\ltimes V_m$, but for some small $m$ this is possible.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We also give a classification of complex perfect Lie algebras $\Lg$ of dimension $n\le 9$ and explicitly compute the cohomology spaces $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ with $k=0,1,2$ for all Lie algebras from the classification list.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $\Lg$ be a finite-dimensional Lie algebra over a field of characteristic zero.
elements.sent-2411-008: If $\Lg$ is semisimple, then there are many vanishing results known on the Lie algebra cohomology.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, by Whitehead's first and second lemma we have $H^1(\Lg,M)=H^2(\Lg,M)=0$ for every finite-dimensional $\Lg$-module $M$.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is natural, to study the cohomology also for {\em perfect Lie algebras, i.e., for Lie algebras $\Lg$ satisfying $[\Lg,\Lg]=\Lg$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: It turns out that the Whitehead lemmas are no longer true for perfect Lie algebras.
elements.sent-2411-012: So the study of the first and second cohomology groups is much more interesting in this case.
elements.sent-2411-013: But so far, nobody found a perfect non-semisimple Lie algebra with vanishing adjoint cohomology, or could prove that such a Lie algebra does not exist.. \\begin{equation}0.2cm] On the other hand, the adjoint cohomology of perfect Lie algebras is interesting in general, and not so much is known about it. We began a study in \cite{BU75}, which we will continue here. There are some explicit results in the literature for particular families, namely for the perfect non-semisimple Lie algebras $\Lg=sl_2(\C)\ltimes V_m$, where $V_m$ is the irreducible representation of $sl_2(\C)$ of dimension $m+1$ with $m\ge 1$. Here the cohomology groups $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ have been determined for $k=0,1,2$, see \cite{RAU,RIC} and the references therein. [0.2cm] In this paper we will extend these results, and compute the adjoint cohomology in various other cases. In particular, for odd $m=2n-1$, we show in Proposition $\ref{3.20}$ that \[ H^3(\Lg,\Lg) \cong \C^{\lfloor \frac{n+1}{3}\rfloor}, \; H^4(\Lg,\Lg) \cong \C.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}0.2cm] On the other hand, the adjoint cohomology of perfect Lie algebras is interesting in general, and not so much is known about it. We began a study in \cite{BU75}, which we will continue here. There are some explicit results in the literature for particular families, namely for the perfect non-semisimple Lie algebras $\Lg=sl_2(\C)\ltimes V_m$, where $V_m$ is the irreducible representation of $sl_2(\C)$ of dimension $m+1$ with $m\ge 1$. Here the cohomology groups $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ have been determined for $k=0,1,2$, see \cite{RAU,RIC} and the references therein. [0.2cm] In this paper we will extend these results, and compute the adjoint cohomology in various other cases. In particular, for odd $m=2n-1$, we show in Proposition $\ref{3.20}$ that \[ H^3(\Lg,\Lg) \cong \C^{\lfloor \frac{n+1}{3}\rfloor}, \; H^4(\Lg,\Lg) \cong \C.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-014: Here we use a long exact sequence in cohomology and compute certain spaces determined by the multiplicities $N(j,k,n)$ of $V_{jk-2n}$ in the exterior product $\Lambda^j(V_{j+k-1 )$ for $j\le 4$.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The numbers $N(j,k,n)$ are given by the coefficient of $q^n$ in the Gaussian $q$-polynomial $(1-q)\genfrac[]{0pt {1 {j+k {k _q$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We have $N(j,k,n)=p(j,k,n)-p(j,k,n-1)$, where $p(j,k,n)$ is the number of partitions of $n$ into at most $k$ parts with largest part at most $j$.
elements.sent-2411-017: We are able to compute some of these multiplicities, so that we can apply the Hochschild-Serre formula to obtain a result on the adjoint cohomology of $\Lg$.
elements.sent-2411-018: For $m=1,2,3,5,7$ we can even compute the total adjoint cohomology of $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)\ltimes V_m$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $\Lg$ be a finite-dimensional complex Lie algebra.
elements.sent-2411-020: Although many results also hold for arbitrary fields of characteristic zero, it is sufficient for our main results to consider only complex Lie algebras.
elements.sent-2411-021: We denote by $\Der(\Lg)$ the Lie algebra of derivations of $\Lg$, and by $\ad(\Lg)$ the ideal of inner derivations in $\Der(\Lg)$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Furthermore, we denote by $Z(\Lg)$ the center of $\Lg$, by $\rad(\Lg)$ the solvable radical of $\Lg$, and by $\nil(\Lg)$ the nilradical of $\Lg$.
elements.sent-2411-023: It is known that the solvable radical of a perfect Lie algebra is nilpotent, see for example Lemma $2.4$ in \cite{BU75 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: We can use the Levi decomposition of a Lie algebra to decide whether or not it is perfect.
elements.sent-2411-026: The following result is well known.
elements.sent-2411-028: Then $\Lg$ is perfect if and only if $V$ does not contain the trivial $1$-dimensional $\Ls$-module.
elements.sent-2411-029: This turns the vector space $\Lg=\Ls \dotplus V$ into a perfect Lie algebra, the semidirect product $\Lg=\Ls\ltimes V$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Consider the Lie algebra $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)$ with basis $(e_1,e_2,e_3)$ and Lie brackets given by \begin{equation}[e_1,e_2]=e_3, \, [e_1,e_3] = -2e_1,\, [e_2,e_3] = 2e_2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $V_m$ be the irreducible representation of dimension $m+1$ with basis $(e_4,\ldots ,e_{m+4 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Also, it has a trivial center.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, note that a perfect Lie algebra may have a non-trivial center in general.
elements.sent-2411-034: For $m=1$ the Lie brackets for $\Lg=\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)\ltimes V_1$ are given by *{0.5cm \begin{equation}[e_1,e_2] = e_3, [e_2,e_3] = 2e_2, [e_3,e_5] =-e_5. [e_1,e_3] = -2e_1, [e_2,e_4] = e_5, [e_1,e_5] = e_4, [e_3,e_4] = e_4,\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this section we want to obtain results on the adjoint cohomology spaces $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ with $k\ge 0$ for the perfect Lie algebras $\Lg=\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)\ltimes V_m$ with $m\ge 1$.}

elements.sent-2411-038: For $k=0$ we have $H^0(\Lg,\Lg)=Z(\Lg)$ for all Lie algebras $\Lg$.

elements.sent-2411-041: Let $\Lg=\Ls\ltimes V$, where $\Ls$ is semisimple and $V$ is an $\Ls$-module.
elements.sent-2411-043: In particular, if $V$ is simple, we have $\dim H^1(\Lg,\Lg)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Consider $V$ as an abelian Lie algebra.
elements.sent-2411-046: Then we have a short exact sequence of $\Lg$-modules \begin{equation}0\ra V\ra \Lg\ra \Lg/V\ra 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is also a short exact sequence of $V$-modules by restriction to $V\subseteq \Lg$.
elements.sent-2411-049: This yields a long exact sequence in cohomology, because the functor of $\Ls$-invariants is exact on finite-dimensional modules.
elements.sent-2411-050: Then we have \begin{equation}0 \to H^0(V,V)^\Ls\to H^0(V,\Lg)^\Ls\to H^0(V,\Lg/V)^\Ls \to H^1(V,V)^\Ls\to H^1(V,\Lg)^\Ls\to H^1(V,\Lg/V)^\Ls \to H^2(V,V)^\Ls\to H^2(V,\Lg)^\Ls\to H^2(V,\Lg/V)^\Ls \to H^3(V,V)^\Ls\to H^3(V,\Lg)^\Ls\to H^3(V,\Lg/V)^\Ls \to \cdots\end{equation} The sequence continues until $k> \dim V$, in which case we have \begin{equation}H^k(V,V)^{\Ls}=H^k(V,\Lg)^\Ls=H^k(V,\Lg/V)^\Ls=0.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-144: Hence by the $4$-term exact sequences of Proposition $\ref{3.19}$ we have \begin{equation}H^k(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \cong  \C,  if  k=1,3,5 0,  if  k=0,2,4 \end{equation} So the Hochschild-Serre formula gives \begin{equation}H^5(\Lg,\Lg) \cong H^5(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \oplus H^2(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \cong \C, H^6(\Lg,\Lg) \cong H^6(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \oplus H^3(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \cong \C, H^7(\Lg,\Lg) \cong H^4(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} =0, H^8(\Lg,\Lg) \cong H^5(V,\Lg)^{\Ls} \cong \C.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Adjoint cohomology for Lie algebras of low dimension
elements.sent-2411-150: In this section we will compute the adjoint cohomology spaces $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ with $k=0,1,2$ for all complex non-semisimple perfect Lie algebras of dimension $n\le 9$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Note that $V_0$ cannot appear as a summand, since we assume that $\Lg$ is perfect.
elements.sent-2411-153: Then $\Lg$ is perfect and we have \begin{equation}\dim H^1(\Lg,\Lg)=e_1^2+\cdots +e_m^2.\end{equation}. \end{prop.}
elements.sent-2411-154: We have \begin{equation}H^1(\Lg,\Lg)\cong\Hom_{sl_2(\C)}(V_{n_1}^{\oplus e_1}\oplus \cdots \oplus V_{n_m}^{\oplus e_m}, V_{n_1}^{\oplus e_1}\oplus \cdots \oplus V_{n_k}^{\oplus e_m}).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-063: \begin{equation}H^1(\Lg,\Lg)\cong\Hom_{sl_2(\C)}(V_{n_1}^{\oplus e_1}\oplus \cdots \oplus V_{n_m}^{\oplus e_m}, V_{n_1}^{\oplus e_1}\oplus \cdots \oplus V_{n_k}^{\oplus e_m}).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-155: Since $V_j$ is a simple $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)$-module, Schur's Lemma implies that this space equals \begin{equation}M_{e_1}(\C)\oplus \cdots \oplus M_{e_m}(\C),\end{equation} which has dimension $e_1^2+\cdots +e_m^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Perfect Lie algebras also can have a solvable radical, which is not abelian.
elements.sent-2411-158: Consider the following semidirect product of $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C)$ and $\Ln_3(\C)$, given by the following Lie brackets in the basis $(e_1,\ldots ,e_6)$: *{0.5cm \begin{equation}[e_1,e_2] = e_3, [e_2,e_3] = 2e_2, [e_3,e_5] =-e_5, [e_1,e_3] = -2e_1, [e_2,e_4] = e_5, [e_4,e_5] = e_6. [e_1,e_5] = e_4, [e_3,e_4] = e_4,\end{equation} We denote this Lie algebra by $\mathfrak{sl _2(\C) \ltimes \Ln_3(\C)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: One can generalize this example to semidirect products $\Ls\ltimes \Ln_{2n+1 (\C)$ for a semisimple Lie algebra $\Ls$ and the Heisenberg Lie algebra $\Ln_{2n+1 (\C)$ of dimension $2n+1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Then $\Lg$ is isomorphic to one of the following algebras, with the adjoint cohomology spaces $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ for $k=0,1,2$ given as follows: *{0.5cm \begin{center \begin{tabular {c|ccccc \color{red {$\Lg$} \color{blue {$\dim \Lg$} \color{green {Turkowski \color{purple {$\dim H^0(\Lg,\Lg)$} \color{orange {$\dim H^1(\Lg,\Lg)$} \color{brown {$\dim H^2(\Lg,\Lg)$} $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_1$ $5$ $L_{5,1}$ $0$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_2$ $6$ $L_{6,4}\cong L_{6,1}$ $0$ $1$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes \Ln_3(\C)$ $6$ $L_{6,2}$ $1$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_3$ $7$ $L_{7,6}$ $0$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus V_1)$ $7$ $L_{7,7}$ $0$ $4$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\oplus (\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_1)$ $8$ $\Ls\Ll_2\oplus L_{5,1}$ $0$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_4$ $8$ $L_{8,21}$ $0$ $1$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus V_2)$ $8$ $L_{8,22}$ $0$ $2$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus \Ln_3(\C))$ $8$ $\color{red {L_{8,13 ^{\ep=0 }$ $1$ $3$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes \Lf_{2,3 (\C)$ $8$ $L_{8,15}$ $0$ $1$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes_{\phi \Ln_5(\C)$ $8$ $L_{8,13 ^1\cong L_{8,13 ^{-1}$ $1$ $2$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes_{\psi \Ln_5(\C)$ $8$ $L_{8,19}$ $1$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\oplus (\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_2)$ $9$ $\Ls\Ll_2\oplus L_{6,1}$ $0$ $1$ $1$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\oplus (\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes \Ln_3(\C))$ $9$ $\Ls\Ll_2\oplus L_{6,2}$ $1$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes V_5$ $9$ $L_{9,59}$ $0$ $1$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus V_3)$ $9$ $L_{9,60}$ $0$ $2$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_2\oplus V_2)$ $9$ $L_{9,61}$ $0$ $4$ $4$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus V_1\oplus V_1)$ $9$ $L_{9,63}$ $0$ $9$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_2\oplus \Ln_3(\C))$ $9$ $L_{9,58}$ $1$ $2$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (\Ln_3(\C)\oplus \Ln_3(\C))$ $9$ $L_{9,37}\cong L_{9,42}$ $2$ $2$ $0$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes \Lf_{3,2 (\C)$ $9$ $L_{9,62}$ $0$ $2$ $2$ $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes \CA_{6,4 (\C)$ $9$ $L_{9,41}$ $2$ $3$ $1$ \end{tabular \end{center \end{prop.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: The classification of perfect Lie algebras in low dimension is based on the the work of Turkowski, who classified Lie algebras with non-trivial Levi decomposition up to dimension $8$ over the real numbers in \cite{TUR1 , and for dimension $9$ over real and complex numbers in \cite{TUR2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-162: He lists explicit Lie brackets for all algebras.
elements.sent-2411-163: From this work it is not difficult to derive a classification of complex perfect Lie algebras of dimension $n\le 9$.
elements.sent-2411-164: We need to add one Lie algebra though, which Turkowski has not in his list.
elements.sent-2411-165: It is the complexification of the algebra $L_{8,13 ^{\ep}$ for $\ep=0$, isomorphic to $\Ls\Ll_2(\C)\ltimes (V_1\oplus \Ln_3(\C))$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: We have computed the adjoint cohomology groups $H^k(\Lg,\Lg)$ for $k=0,1,2$ in each case by computer, and have compared the results for the cases covered by Proposition $\ref{5.1}$.
